title: Matelea patalensis
intro: Matelea patalensis, also known as the "gordolobo," is a plant species native to Mexico and Central America, belonging to the Apocynaceae family. Traditionally, Matelea patalensis has been used for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which may aid in treating wounds, reducing fever, and alleviating respiratory issues such as bronchitis and asthma. The bioactive constituents of Matelea patalensis include alkaloids, flavonoids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Matelea patalensis is commonly prepared as a tea or infusion, as well as being used in topical applications such as salves and poultices.
taxonomy: Matelea patalensis is classified within the Apocynaceae family of plants, which includes a diverse range of species commonly known as dogbanes, oleanders, and periwinkles. This family is further divided into 49 subfamilies and 350 genera. Matelea patalensis itself is a member of the Matelea genus, which is native to the Americas. The Matelea genus consists of around 140 species of tropical and subtropical plants. Matelea patalensis is a climbing plant that belongs to the Asclepiadoideae subfamily within the Apocynaceae family.
uses: Matelea patalensis has been reported to have medicinal properties, although its uses may vary depending on the region and cultural context. Traditional healers in certain areas use Matelea patalensis to treat various ailments, including fever, respiratory issues, and skin conditions. More research is needed to confirm the effectiveness and safety of using Matelea patalensis for medicinal purposes.
